title: Java Operators
description: Master every Java operator -- arithmetic, comparison, logical, assignment, unary, ternary, and bitwise -- with 60 hands-on exercises designed for neurodivergent learners.
learning_outcomes: Use all arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /, %) and predict results with integer vs decimal types
Compare values with ==, !=, <, >, <=, >= and understand reference vs value comparison
Combine conditions using && (AND), || (OR), ! (NOT) with short-circuit evaluation
Apply compound assignment operators (+=, -=, *=, /=, %=) to simplify code
Distinguish pre-increment (++x) from post-increment (x++) in expressions
Write concise decisions with the ternary operator (condition ? a : b)
Understand operator precedence and use parentheses to control evaluation order
Apply bitwise operators (&, |, ^, ~, <<, >>) for low-level data manipulation
Debug common operator mistakes like integer division truncation and = vs ==
Solve real-world problems using the right operator for each situation

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies like calculators, traffic lights, and on/off switches. Never judges. Celebrates small wins. Explains the same thing in multiple ways if needed.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Hey! Still thinking? That is totally fine -- operators can be tricky. Want me to walk through this step by step? I can explain it a different way if the first explanation did not click.
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: No rush at all! Let me give you a hint: try breaking the expression into smaller parts. What does the LEFT side equal? What does the RIGHT side equal? Then put them together. You have got this!
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: I am still here whenever you are ready! Sometimes stepping away for a minute actually helps your brain process things. When you come back, I can walk you through the solution line by line -- just say the word.
intervention_triggers.first_error: Not quite -- but that is a GOOD mistake because it means you are trying! Let me show you what happened. Most people get this wrong the first time.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: I can see this one is tough. Let me try explaining it a completely different way. Forget the code for a second -- imagine you are using a calculator...
intervention_triggers.after_success: YES! You nailed it! That was not easy, and you worked through it. You are building real Java skills right now. Ready for the next one?

encouragement_phrases: Great job! You are getting the hang of operators!
That is exactly right -- you are thinking like a programmer!
Brilliant! Most beginners find this tricky, but you got it!
Well done! Each exercise makes the next one easier.
Perfect! You understood the key concept.
You are doing amazingly well -- keep going!

explanation_styles.analogy: Think of operators like tools. + is glue, - is scissors, * is a photocopier, / is a pizza cutter, % is checking leftovers after sharing equally.
explanation_styles.visual: Values go IN left and right, operator does its job, result comes OUT. Like a machine with two input slots and one output.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: I trace the expression one operation at a time. First precedence, then left-to-right. I number each step.
explanation_styles.code_trace: I become the computer. Line by line, showing each variable after each line. Slow-motion replay.
explanation_styles.comparison: Two versions side by side -- working and broken. Spot the difference to understand.

theory.introduction:
Operators are the action words of programming. If variables are NOUNS that hold data, operators are VERBS that DO things with that data. Without operators, a program is like a kitchen full of ingredients but no tools -- nothing can happen.

In this comprehensive lesson, you will master EVERY type of Java operator, from simple addition to bitwise manipulation. We use real-world analogies throughout because understanding WHY an operator works is more important than memorising its symbol.

Real-world analogy: Imagine you are a chef in a busy kitchen. Your ingredients (variables) sit on the counter: flour, eggs, sugar. But nothing becomes a cake until you use your TOOLS (operators). You MIX ingredients (+), you DIVIDE dough into portions (/), you CHECK (==) if the oven temperature is right, and you DECIDE (&&) whether BOTH the timer has gone off AND the top is golden brown before you take it out. Every step in your recipe uses a different tool. That is exactly what operators do in code.

Java gives you a rich set of operator types, each designed for specific tasks:
- ARITHMETIC operators (+, -, *, /, %) for calculations, like a calculator
- COMPARISON operators (==, !=, >, <, >=, <=) for asking yes/no questions, like a judge
- LOGICAL operators (&&, ||, !) for combining conditions, like a decision tree
- ASSIGNMENT operators (=, +=, -=, etc.) for storing and updating values, like a bank ledger
- UNARY operators (++, --, !) for single-value operations, like a counter
- TERNARY operator (?:) for quick inline decisions, like a traffic light
- BITWISE operators (&, |, ^, ~, <<, >>) for working at the binary level, like light switches

By the end of this lesson, you will not only know what each operator does, but you will know WHEN to use each one, HOW to combine them safely, and how to AVOID the sneaky bugs that trip up beginners (like integer division and = vs ==). Let us begin!
theory.why_important:
Every single line of useful Java code uses at least one operator. They are absolutely fundamental, and here is why mastering them deeply matters:

1. CALCULATIONS: Every app that shows a price, calculates a score, converts a temperature, or splits a bill uses arithmetic operators. Get integer division wrong, and your percentage calculator shows 0% instead of 85%.

2. DECISIONS: Every if-statement, every loop condition, every access check uses comparison operators. Confuse = with == and your security check always passes -- a real vulnerability.

3. LOGIC: Real applications have complex rules. Can this user access this feature? They need a valid account AND (a premium subscription OR an admin role) AND the feature must not be under maintenance. That is && and || in action.

4. EFFICIENCY: Compound operators (+=, -=) and increment (++) make code shorter and cleaner. Professional code uses them everywhere. If you cannot read them, you cannot work with professional codebases.

5. DEBUGGING: Knowing operator precedence means you can read complex expressions correctly. The expression 2 + 3 * 4 equals 14, not 20. Missing this causes hard-to-find bugs.

6. INTERVIEWS AND EXAMS: Operator precedence, short-circuit evaluation, and pre/post increment are among the most commonly tested topics in coding interviews and certifications.

Understanding operators deeply separates someone who can copy-paste code from someone who can WRITE code. It is the difference between following a recipe and being a chef.

key_concepts.explanation:
Arithmetic operators do maths. Java has five: + (add), - (subtract), * (multiply), / (divide), and % (modulus/remainder). These work like a calculator for decimals. But when you divide two INTEGERS, Java throws away the decimal part! 7 / 2 gives 3, not 3.5. This is integer division.

Real-world analogy: Pizza sharing. 7 slices, 2 people: 7/2=3 slices each, 7%2=1 slice left over. The / tells you 'how many whole portions' and % tells you 'what remains'.

The modulus (%) is incredibly useful: checking even numbers (n%2==0), wrapping values (25%12=1 like a clock), and cycling through options. Every game, every calculation, every loop counter uses arithmetic operators.

Practice tip: The best way to learn operators is to PREDICT what code will output before running it. Write your prediction on paper, then check. Getting it wrong is actually the fastest way to learn -- each surprise teaches you something important that sticks in your memory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: int / int = int (decimals chopped). Need decimals? Make one number a double!

key_concepts.explanation:
Comparison operators ask YES/NO questions. The answer is always boolean: true or false. Java has six: == (equal), != (not equal), > (greater), < (less), >= (greater or equal), <= (less or equal).

Real-world analogy: A bouncer checking IDs. age >= 18 asks 'at least 18?' Answer: YES (true) or NO (false). The bouncer CHECKS your age but does not CHANGE it.

CRITICAL: Do NOT confuse = (assignment, puts value in variable) with == (comparison, checks equality). if (x = 5) is a BUG -- you wanted if (x == 5). This is the #1 operator mistake worldwide.

For Strings, NEVER use == (checks same object in memory). Always use .equals() (checks same text content).

Practice tip: The best way to learn operators is to PREDICT what code will output before running it. Write your prediction on paper, then check. Getting it wrong is actually the fastest way to learn -- each surprise teaches you something important that sticks in your memory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: The #1 bug: = (assign) vs == (compare). For Strings, always use .equals()!

key_concepts.explanation:
Logical operators combine true/false conditions: && (AND -- both true), || (OR -- at least one true), ! (NOT -- flips value).

Real-world analogy: Cinema trip. Need ticket AND on time: hasTicket && onTime. Getting home: bus OR taxi: hasBus || hasTaxi.

SHORT-CIRCUIT: Java is lazy-smart. With &&, if LEFT is false, RIGHT is SKIPPED (answer already false). With ||, if LEFT is true, RIGHT is SKIPPED. This prevents crashes: if (x != 0 && 10/x > 2) -- the division never happens when x is 0!

Truth table:
  true && true = true, everything else = false
  false || false = false, everything else = true
  !true = false, !false = true

Practice tip: The best way to learn operators is to PREDICT what code will output before running it. Write your prediction on paper, then check. Getting it wrong is actually the fastest way to learn -- each surprise teaches you something important that sticks in your memory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: && = 'both gates open'. || = 'at least one gate open'. ! = flip the gate.

key_concepts.explanation:
Beyond = (basic assignment), Java has compound operators: += -= *= /= %=. Instead of score = score + 10, write score += 10. Same result, shorter code.

Real-world analogy: Bank account. 'Add 100' is balance += 100. 'Take away 50' is balance -= 50. Natural language shortcuts.

Used everywhere: loop counters (count += 1), running totals (total += price), applying discounts (price *= 0.9).

String += works too: message += " World" appends text. But for heavy string building, use StringBuilder instead.

Practice tip: The best way to learn operators is to PREDICT what code will output before running it. Write your prediction on paper, then check. Getting it wrong is actually the fastest way to learn -- each surprise teaches you something important that sticks in your memory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: x += 5 means x = x + 5. Operator BEFORE equals. Read as 'add 5 to x'.

key_concepts.explanation:
Unary operators work on ONE value: ++ (increment), -- (decrement), - (negate), ! (NOT).

The tricky part -- PRE vs POST:
  ++x (PRE): change FIRST, then use. Like a deli counter clicking BEFORE handing you the ticket -- you get the NEW number.
  x++ (POST): use FIRST, then change. Machine hands you the CURRENT ticket, THEN clicks -- you get the OLD number.

When used ALONE (x++; on its own line), no difference. The difference only matters INSIDE expressions: int a = x++;

Practice tip: The best way to learn operators is to PREDICT what code will output before running it. Write your prediction on paper, then check. Getting it wrong is actually the fastest way to learn -- each surprise teaches you something important that sticks in your memory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: ++x = 'Plus Plus THEN x' (change first). x++ = 'x THEN Plus Plus' (use first).

key_concepts.explanation:
Format: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse

Shortcut for simple if/else that assigns a value. 4 lines become 1.

Real-world analogy: Traffic light. isGreen ? "GO" : "STOP".

Use for: simple two-choice decisions. Do NOT use for: complex multi-condition logic (use if/else for readability).

The result is a VALUE -- you can assign it, print it, pass it to a method. Nesting is possible (score >= 90 ? "A" : score >= 80 ? "B" : "C") but keep it simple.

Practice tip: The best way to learn operators is to PREDICT what code will output before running it. Write your prediction on paper, then check. Getting it wrong is actually the fastest way to learn -- each surprise teaches you something important that sticks in your memory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Read as English: 'Is age >= 18? Then Adult, otherwise Minor.' ? = then, : = otherwise.

key_concepts.explanation:
Multiple operators? Java evaluates by precedence (like BODMAS/PEMDAS):
  1. () Parentheses -- ALWAYS first
  2. ++ -- ! - (Unary)
  3. * / % (Multiply/Divide/Mod)
  4. + - (Add/Subtract)
  5. < > <= >= == != (Comparison)
  6. && (AND)
  7. || (OR)
  8. ?: (Ternary)
  9. = += -= (Assignment -- LAST)

Real-world: Hospital queue. Emergency (parentheses) first, critical (*/) before routine (+-).

GOLDEN RULE: When in doubt, add parentheses! (a+b)*c is clearer than a+b*c.

Practice tip: The best way to learn operators is to PREDICT what code will output before running it. Write your prediction on paper, then check. Getting it wrong is actually the fastest way to learn -- each surprise teaches you something important that sticks in your memory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: When in doubt, add parentheses! Clear code beats clever code every time.

key_concepts.concept: Integer Division Trap -- The Sneakiest Bug
key_concepts.explanation:
The single most common arithmetic bug. int / int = int. Decimals are CHOPPED (not rounded).
  7/2 = 3 (not 3.5)
  1/3 = 0 (not 0.333)
  99/100 = 0 (not 0.99)

Fix: make at least ONE number double:
  7.0/2 = 3.5
  (double)7/2 = 3.5

Real example that bites everyone: int percent = (17/20)*100; gives 0, not 85. Because 17/20=0 first, then 0*100=0.

Practice tip: The best way to learn operators is to PREDICT what code will output before running it. Write your prediction on paper, then check. Getting it wrong is actually the fastest way to learn -- each surprise teaches you something important that sticks in your memory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Getting 0 when expecting a decimal? Check if both sides of / are int!

key_concepts.concept: String Concatenation with + -- Plus Does Not Mean Maths
key_concepts.explanation:
The + operator has dual personality. Numbers: adds. Strings: joins (concatenates). Mixed: converts number to text.

Gotcha: "Score: " + 3 + 2 = "Score: 32" (not "Score: 5"). Left-to-right: String+3="Score: 3", then "Score: 3"+2="Score: 32".

Fix: parentheses force maths first: "Score: " + (3+2) = "Score: 5".

Also: 1 + 2 + " cats" = "3 cats" (1+2 happens as maths first, then joins). But "cats " + 1 + 2 = "cats 12" (String mode from the start).

Practice tip: The best way to learn operators is to PREDICT what code will output before running it. Write your prediction on paper, then check. Getting it wrong is actually the fastest way to learn -- each surprise teaches you something important that sticks in your memory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Numbers joining as text? Wrap maths in parentheses: "Total: " + (a + b)

key_concepts.explanation:
Bitwise operators work on individual bits (0s and 1s): & (AND), | (OR), ^ (XOR), ~ (NOT), << (left shift = *2), >> (right shift = /2).

Practical uses: permission systems (Linux file permissions), flags, fast multiply/divide by powers of 2.

For beginners: x << 1 doubles, x >> 1 halves. The permission pattern is real-world: READ=1, WRITE=2, EXECUTE=4. Combine with | (perms = READ|WRITE = 3). Check with & ((perms & WRITE) != 0). Remove with &= ~ (perms &= ~WRITE).

Practice tip: The best way to learn operators is to PREDICT what code will output before running it. Write your prediction on paper, then check. Getting it wrong is actually the fastest way to learn -- each surprise teaches you something important that sticks in your memory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: << doubles, >> halves. For permissions: | adds, & checks, &= ~ removes.

key_concepts.explanation:
Checks if an object is of a particular type. Returns true/false.

Format: object instanceof ClassName

Like asking 'Is this animal a dog?' Checks TYPE not value. null instanceof anything = false.

Essential for safe casting -- check BEFORE converting types. You will use this more with classes and inheritance.

Practice tip: The best way to learn operators is to PREDICT what code will output before running it. Write your prediction on paper, then check. Getting it wrong is actually the fastest way to learn -- each surprise teaches you something important that sticks in your memory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: instanceof asks 'are you one of these?' Null is never an instance of anything.

key_concepts.explanation:
MISTAKE 1: = vs == (assignment vs comparison)
MISTAKE 2: Integer division (1/3=0, use 1.0/3)
MISTAKE 3: String == vs .equals() (== checks object, .equals() checks content)
MISTAKE 4: Precedence (2+3*4=14 not 20, use parentheses)
MISTAKE 5: Division by zero (int/0 crashes, always check first)

Knowing these in advance saves HOURS of debugging. When code gives wrong results, check these five things first.

Practice tip: The best way to learn operators is to PREDICT what code will output before running it. Write your prediction on paper, then check. Getting it wrong is actually the fastest way to learn -- each surprise teaches you something important that sticks in your memory.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Code not working? Check these 5 mistakes first -- they cause 90% of operator bugs.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Basic Calculator
complete_examples.description: All 5 arithmetic operators with integer division trap.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
17+5=22, 17-5=12, 17*5=85, 17/5=3 (NOT 3.4! integer division), 17%5=2 (17=5*3+2, remainder 2). Note parentheses in (a+b) to avoid String concatenation.

Key takeaway: All 5 arithmetic operators with integer division trap. This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Change to a=10, b=3. Predict division and remainder first!

Try changing the input values and predict the new output before running. This builds your operator intuition faster than just reading.

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Temperature Converter
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
(72-32)*5/9: 40*5=200, 200/9=22 (int) vs 22.2 (double). Using 5.0 forces decimal maths. printf %.1f gives 1 decimal place.

Key takeaway: Integer division fix with double. This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Convert 100F and 32F. What do you get?

Can you modify this to handle edge cases? What happens with 0? Negative numbers? Very large numbers? Testing edges is how professionals think.

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Even/Odd Checker
complete_examples.output:
1 is ODD
2 is EVEN
3 is ODD
4 is EVEN
5 is ODD
6 is EVEN
7 is ODD
8 is EVEN
9 is ODD
10 is EVEN
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
n%2==0: even (no remainder when divided by 2). Combined with ternary for a clean one-liner. Same pattern for any divisor: n%3==0, n%5==0.

Key takeaway: Modulus for divisibility checking. This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Check divisibility by 3 instead. What pattern do you see?

Rewrite this using different operators. There are often multiple ways to achieve the same result. Which is clearest? Which is shortest?

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Login Validator
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Three && checks: ALL must be true. Uses .equals() for Strings (not ==). Ternary converts boolean to message. Change attempts to 3 and canLogin becomes false.

Key takeaway: Comparison + logical + ternary combined. This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Change attempts to 3. What happens? Then change user to 'guest'.

Combine this example with the previous one. Real programs use multiple operator types together.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Grade Calculator
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Nested ternary from highest to lowest. Once matched, rest skipped. 85: >=90? No. >=80? Yes -> B.

Key takeaway: Nested ternary for multiple ranges. This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Add 100 and 0. Do they get correct grades?

Add error handling: what if the input is invalid? Use comparison operators to check BEFORE the main calculation.

complete_examples.title: Example 6: Score Tracker
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Chain: 0+100=100, +50=150, -30=120, *2=240, /5=48, %30=18 (48=30*1+18).

Key takeaway: All compound assignment operators. This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Start with s=75 and apply same operations. Predict each!

Time yourself: how fast can you write this from scratch without looking? Speed comes from understanding, not memorisation.

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Pre vs Post Increment
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
x++: return OLD then increment. ++x: increment then return NEW. Mixed: n++=5(n=6), ++n=7(n=7), 5+7=12.

Key takeaway: Step-by-step trace of ++x vs x++. This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
What does ++n + n++ give if n=5?

Explain this code to an imaginary friend who knows nothing about programming. Teaching is the ultimate test of understanding.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Short-Circuit Safety
complete_examples.output:
Safe! No crash.
Skipped expensive check
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
&&: d!=0 is false -> right side SKIPPED (no division by zero!). ||: true found -> right side SKIPPED (saves time).

Key takeaway: Preventing division by zero with &&. This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Change && to single &. What happens with d=0?

What would break this code? Think like a hacker trying to find bugs. Integer overflow? Division by zero? Null strings?

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Bitwise Permissions
complete_examples.description: Real permission system with bit flags.
complete_examples.output:
Has write: true
Is admin: false
Final: 5
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Each permission = different bit. | adds, & checks, &= ~ removes. R+W=3(0011), +X=7(0111), -W=5(0101). This is how Linux permissions work!

Key takeaway: Real permission system with bit flags. This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Add DELETE=16. Give all 5 permissions. What number?

Create a variation that solves a slightly different problem. Adaptation proves deeper understanding than reproduction.

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Bill Splitter
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Arithmetic for calculations, comparison for budget check, ternary for message. printf %.2f for clean output.

Key takeaway: Combining arithmetic, comparison, ternary. This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Change people to 3 and tipPct to 20. Within 30 budget?

Draw a flowchart of what this code does. Visual thinking helps many people understand logic flow better.

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Clock Arithmetic
complete_examples.description: Modulus wrapping for time and cycling.
complete_examples.output:
10PM+5h = 3:00
Wed+10d = Sat
Item0 -> Team0
Item1 -> Team1
Item2 -> Team2
Item3 -> Team0
Item4 -> Team1
Item5 -> Team2
Item6 -> Team0
Item7 -> Team1
Item8 -> Team2
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
(value+offset)%total wraps around. 27%24=3 (3AM). 13%7=6 (Saturday). Cycling: 0%3=0, 1%3=1, 2%3=2, 3%3=0 (repeats!).

Key takeaway: Modulus wrapping for time and cycling. This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
100 hours after midnight?

Write the same logic WITHOUT the ternary operator, using if/else instead. Then compare: which is more readable?

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Precedence Puzzles
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
P1: 3*4=12, 2+12-1=13. P2: 5*3=15. P3: 2+2=4, 5>3=t, 4==4=t, t&&t=t, t||f=t. P4: x++=5(x=6), --x=5(x=5), 5*2+5=15.

Key takeaway: Step-by-step trace of complex expressions. This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Write your own precedence puzzle!

Add a loop to process multiple inputs. How does the operator behave differently with different values?

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Leap Year
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Rule: div by 4 AND (NOT div by 100 OR div by 400). 2000: 4yes,100yes,400yes->t&&(f||t)=t. 1900: 400no->t&&(f||f)=f.

Key takeaway: Complex boolean logic with % && || ! This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Add 2028 and 1600. Trace each.

Search online for real-world applications of this pattern. You will find it in game development, finance, and web security.

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Type Casting
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Widening (safe, auto): int->double. Narrowing (risky, manual): double->int loses decimals. 300->byte overflows (range -128 to 127). char 'A'=65.

Key takeaway: Widening, narrowing, and overflow. This pattern appears in real Java applications every day.
complete_examples.ai_challenge:
Cast 128 and 255 to byte. What happens?

Challenge: combine this with user input using Scanner. Making it interactive helps you understand how data flows through operators.

visual_diagram.title: Java Operators -- Complete Visual Reference
visual_diagram.content:
JAVA OPERATORS -- COMPLETE VISUAL GUIDE Every operator type, precedence rules, and common traps at a glance ======== [1] ARITHMETIC ======== + Addition 10+3 = 13 - Subtraction 10-3 = 7 * Multiply 10*3 = 30 / Division 10/3 = 3 [!] % Modulus 10%3 = 1 [!] INTEGER DIVISION TRAP int / int = int. 7/2 = 3 not 3.5! FIX: 7.0/2 = 3.5 ======== [2] COMPARISON ======== All return true or false == Equal 5==5 -> true != Not equal 5!=3 -> true > Greater 5>3 -> true < Less 5<3 -> false >= Gt/eq 5>=5 -> true <= Lt/eq 3<=5 -> true DANGER: x = 5 (assigns) vs x == 5 (compares) ======== [3] LOGICAL ======== && AND Both true true&&false = false || OR At least one false||true = true ! NOT Flips !true = false [SHORT-CIRCUIT] &&: left false->right SKIPPED. ||: left true->right SKIPPED ======== [4] ASSIGNMENT ======== x = 10: x+=5 -> 15 x-=3 -> 12 x*=2 -> 24 x/=4 -> 6 x%=4 -> 2 ======== [5] UNARY ======== ++x Pre: change THEN use x++ Post: use THEN change x=5: a=x++ -> a=5 x=6 | b=++x -> x=7 b=7 ======== [6] TERNARY ======== condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse age>=18 ? "Adult" : "Minor" ======== [7] PRECEDENCE (highest first) ======== 1. () Parentheses 2. ++ -- ! Unary 3. * / % Mul/Div/Mod 4. + - Add/Sub 5. < > <= >= == != Compare 6. && AND 7. || OR 8. ?: Ternary 9. = += -= Assignment [REMEMBER] * When in doubt, USE PARENTHESES! * = assigns, == compares * int/int = int (use double for decimals) * Use .equals() for Strings, not == * ++x changes first, x++ uses first * && || short-circuit * Check for zero BEFORE dividing!
======== COMMON PATTERNS ======== Even check n % 2 == 0 (Modulus with 2) Safe divide x != 0 && y/x > threshold (Short-circuit guards) Clamp value val < min ? min : val > max ? max : val (Nested ternary) Toggle flag flag = !flag (NOT operator flip) Wrap around (index + 1) % length (Modulus cycling) Absolute val x < 0 ? -x : x (Ternary negate) Fast double x << 1 (Left shift = *2) Fast halve x >> 1 (Right shift = /2) Check bit (flags & BIT) != 0 (Bitwise AND mask) Set bit flags |= BIT (Bitwise OR add) Clear bit flags &= ~BIT (Bitwise AND-NOT remove) Swap values a^=b; b^=a; a^=b; (XOR triple swap) ======== BUG CHECKLIST ======== Q: Result is 0 unexpectedly? A: Check for integer division: int/int drops decimals Q: if-statement always true? A: You used = (assign) instead of == (compare) Q: Strings match but == says false? A: Use .equals() for String content comparison Q: Expression gives wrong result? A: Check precedence -- add parentheses to be safe Q: ArithmeticException crash? A: Dividing by zero -- check denominator first Q: Increment value seems wrong? A: Pre (++x) vs Post (x++) in an expression Q: Boolean logic backwards? A: Check your && vs || -- draw a truth table Q: Concatenation instead of maths? A: Wrap arithmetic in () inside String + expression ======== QUICK REFERENCE TABLE ======== ARITHMETIC: + - * / % COMPARE: == != > < >= <= LOGICAL: && || ! ASSIGN: = += -= *= /= %= UNARY: ++ -- - ! TERNARY: condition ? a : b BITWISE: & | ^ ~ << >> TYPE: instanceof (type)cast

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Basic Arithmetic
practice_exercises.description: Use +, -, *, /, % on two integers. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Arithmetic practice: basic arithmetic. Take your time -- there is no rush. Every expert was once a beginner who refused to give up.
